title: Trajectory Dissociation as Operator Freezing
subtitle: Why LLMs Can't Switch Primitives Mid-Stream

children.children: What if the reason AI-generated text feels 'correct but lifeless' isn't about individual word choices at all — but about the model being FROZEN on a meaning manifold? This tutorial explores the trajectory dissociation: a model that can speak fluently but never changes what it's talking ABOUT, and how this maps onto Schank's operator theory.

Human speech is restless. In a single paragraph, you might narrate an event, drop into sarcasm, quote someone else's words, shift to technical jargon, then return to casual register — all without breaking stride. Each of these shifts is a transition between different regions of a 'meaning manifold': a high-dimensional space where nearby points represent similar communicative states.

The Hydra architecture (a multi-head linguistic preprocessing system built on fiber bundle geometry) achieved something remarkable: it resolved the 'fluency dissociation' where single-head models could learn structure but only generate single tokens. With all four heads active, Hydra generates coherent multi-word text. But it revealed a NEW problem: the generated text is grammatically correct but manifold-static. It never transitions between thematic roles, never shifts information structure. It's stuck.

Imagine a musician who has learned to play every chord on the guitar perfectly — crisp fingering, beautiful tone, perfect rhythm. But they only ever play one chord per song. The chord might be gorgeous. The rhythm might be impeccable. But the song has no harmonic movement. No tension, no resolution, no surprise. Human music — and human speech — lives in the TRANSITIONS between chords, not in any single chord. The trajectory dissociation is exactly this: a model that plays beautiful chords but never changes them.
The Hydra project discovered dissociations in two stages. Understanding both is key to seeing why operator freezing is such a fundamental problem.

items.definition: Single-head models (phrase structure only, or phrase structure + thematic roles) learn structural patterns perfectly — 100% center-embedding accuracy at all depths — but generate only single tokens. Average output: 1.0 word. The model has learned the coordinate system but can't produce language. Structure without fluency.

The all-heads model (phrase structure + thematic roles + information structure + discovery head) generates coherent multi-word text: 42 words average, 0.90 distinct-2 bigram diversity. But the generated text has zero thematic transitions, near-zero information structure transitions, and 60× lower 'trajectory energy' than human text. Fluency without movement.

children.children: The trajectory dissociation may be INVISIBLE in standard language models. LLMs generate plausible text, so we don't notice the frozen trajectory. But 'AI slop' — text that is correct but feels lifeless — could be exactly this phenomenon at scale: models that predict within an operator but have no mechanism for the higher-order 'which operator next?' decision.

props.title: Fiber Trajectories: What Human Text Looks Like

children.children: In the fiber bundle framework, each token in a sentence occupies a position on multiple 'fibers' — independent interpretive dimensions like syntactic role, thematic role, and discourse status. A sentence traces a trajectory through this multi-fiber space.

props.title: Trace the Fiber Trajectory
props.prompt: For each sentence, identify the fiber transitions — moments where the thematic role, register, or information structure shifts between adjacent words or clauses.

scenarios.input: "So the doctor — and this guy was, like, OLD SCHOOL — he looks at the chart and goes, 'Well, technically your levels are within range.'"
scenarios.reveal: At least 5 fiber transitions: (1) Narrative frame → (2) Colloquial aside ('this guy was, like') → (3) Back to narrative ('he looks at the chart') → (4) Performed clinical dialogue ('technically your levels...') → (5) The hedge register within the quote ('Well, technically'). Human speech is constantly hopping between manifold neighborhoods.

scenarios.input: "The defendant, pursuant to Section 12(b), kicked the bucket on or about March 15."
scenarios.reveal: Legal register maintained throughout EXCEPT for 'kicked the bucket' — a colloquial idiom that makes a momentary excursion along the register fiber while the syntactic and thematic fibers stay in legalese. This is a single-fiber transition, like a musician playing one chromatic note before returning to the key.

scenarios.input: "If Napoleon had won at Waterloo, Europe would look different today."
scenarios.reveal: Modal fiber transition: 'Napoleon' starts in the actual world → 'had won' moves into the counterfactual → 'Waterloo' is actual (the place exists) → 'Europe would look different' is counterfactual consequence → 'today' partially returns to actuality. The modal fiber oscillates within a single sentence.

children.children: Hydra's trajectory analysis measured these transitions quantitatively. The results were stark:

SRL (thematic role) transition frequency: Human = 0.166, Generated = 0.000. Information structure transition frequency: Human = 0.569, Generated = 0.006. Knight ratio (multi-axis simultaneous transitions): Human = 0.119, Generated = 0.000. Energy per token: Human = 0.360, Generated = 0.006. The model is essentially frozen on the manifold — grammatically alive but semantically static.
Why is Hydra frozen? The answer connects to a deep question in cognitive science: how do humans decide when one 'event' ends and another begins?

Zacks and colleagues developed Event Segmentation Theory (EST): humans continuously maintain internal models of the current situation, and event boundaries occur when these models fail — when the current 'operator' no longer predicts what's happening.
Khemlani et al. (eLife 2025) showed that the brain uses at least TWO distinct mechanisms for detecting event boundaries:

items.definition: Something unexpected happens. The current event model predicted X, but Y occurred. This is a REACTIVE signal — you detect the boundary AFTER the surprising event. Example: you're listening to a lecture and suddenly the fire alarm goes off. High prediction error → event boundary → switch to a new operator (evacuation script).

The current event model becomes less certain about what will happen next, BEFORE anything surprising actually occurs. This is a PROACTIVE signal — you detect the boundary because your model is losing confidence. Example: a conversation starts trailing off into awkward silence. Nothing 'wrong' happened, but uncertainty rises → you sense the event (conversation) is ending.

props.title: Why Neither Mechanism Fires for Hydra

children.children: Here's the critical insight: Hydra's generated text has LOW prediction error (the text is grammatically correct and locally coherent, so the model is not surprised by its own output) AND LOW prediction uncertainty (word-level perplexity is 2.9 — the model is extremely confident about what comes next). Neither event-boundary trigger fires. The model never receives a signal that says 'the current operator is done, switch to a new one.' It's like a musician who is so confident in their current chord that they never feel the urge to change it.

This maps directly onto Schankian operator theory. In Schank's Conceptual Dependency framework, primitives like ATRANS (abstract transfer of possession), PTRANS (physical transfer of location), and MTRANS (mental transfer of information) are the basic operators of event comprehension. A narrative is a sequence of operator applications. The trajectory dissociation means Hydra can instantiate an operator — it can generate text 'within' one — but it never fires the transition to a new operator.

We can now state the hypothesis precisely: the trajectory dissociation IS operator freezing. The model has learned to generate tokens within the scope of a single Schankian operator but has no mechanism for operator transitions.

props.title: Spot the Operator Transitions
props.prompt: In each passage, identify where one Schankian operator ends and another begins. Then ask: what signal tells the READER that a transition occurred?

scenarios.input: "John gave Mary the book. She read it on the train home."
scenarios.reveal: ATRANS(John, book, John→Mary) transitions to MTRANS(Mary, book-contents, book→Mary's-mind) at the sentence boundary. The signal? A new agent (She), a new action type (read vs. gave), and a new setting (train vs. implied co-location). Three fibers shift simultaneously — this is a 'knight move' on the meaning manifold.

scenarios.input: "The chef prepared the meal with care, then the waiter carried it to table six, where the critic tasted the first bite and frowned."
scenarios.reveal: Three operator transitions in one sentence: MBUILD(chef, meal-plan) → PTRANS(waiter, meal, kitchen→table) → MTRANS(critic, taste-impression, meal→mind) + MSTATE(critic, displeasure). Each transition changes the agent, the action type, AND the thematic frame. A frozen model might generate 'The chef prepared the meal... the chef prepared another meal... the chef prepared...' — never switching operators.

scenarios.input: "I was just sitting there reading when the phone rang and it was my mother saying Dad had fallen."
scenarios.reveal: ATTEND(I, book) → interrupted by PTRANS(sound, phone→ear) → MTRANS(mother, news, mother→I) → PTRANS(Dad, standing→ground). Four operators in rapid succession, each nested inside the previous. The 'Dad had fallen' is a reported event embedded inside a communication embedded inside an interruption. Human narrative does this effortlessly; a frozen model would stay in the first operator forever.

children.children: Natural language generation resembles a knight's tour in chess more than a rook's path. The knight always moves along TWO axes simultaneously — it cannot move along only one. Natural speech shares this property: each transition typically shifts register AND advances the topic AND adjusts the modal stance, all at once.

props.title: Rook vs. Knight: Two Modes of Generation

children.children: A rook moves in straight lines — smooth, efficient, axis-aligned. Current LLMs generate rook-like text: they advance one fiber at a time, smoothly, predictably. A knight moves in L-shapes — always hopping across two dimensions at once, creating patterns that are locally surprising but globally purposeful. Human speech is knight-like: register shifts coincide with topic advances, modal transitions align with emotional beats. The 'knight ratio' (fraction of transitions that are multi-axis) was 0.119 for human text and exactly 0.000 for Hydra's output. The model has never made a single knight move.

Think of a multi-fiber trajectory as a musical score. The thematic fiber (who does what to whom) is the bass line — it changes infrequently but fundamentally, establishing harmonic center. The information structure fiber (given/new, topic/focus) is the saxophone — moving rapidly, weaving ornamental patterns over the thematic foundation. The fibers are harmonious but not locked together. They coincide on certain beats (the 'knight moments') but each follows its own rhythm.

A text with no fiber transitions is a song stuck on one chord. It may be in key (grammatical). It may use the right instruments (appropriate vocabulary). But it has no harmonic movement. Genre becomes trajectory template: academic writing is a slow classical piece with prepared modulations; casual speech is bebop with rapid chromaticism; legal text is a pedal tone. The 'voice' of a writer is their characteristic harmonic style.

The diagnosis suggests a concrete fix: just as Hydra needed all four fiber heads to break the fluency dissociation, it may need an explicit 'operator transition' head to break the trajectory dissociation.

items.definition: Add a fifth Hydra head that explicitly marks where one Schankian operator ends and another begins. Train it on narrative data annotated with event boundaries (using EST-inspired annotation or ATOMIC event chains). The head fires a special [OP_TRANSITION] token when it detects an operator boundary, giving the model an explicit signal to shift fiber coordinates.

Head 4 (the blank discovery head) was designed to learn whatever dimension maximally reduces prediction error. If operator transitions are the missing dimension, the discovery head might learn to mark them — if given the right training signal. This is the more elegant approach: let the model discover that transitions matter, rather than telling it.

Instead of adding a new head, modify the training objective to reward not only correct next-token prediction but appropriate fiber transitions. Penalize long runs with zero transition. Reward 'knight moves' (multi-axis transitions). This teaches the model chord progressions, not just which notes are in key.

props.title: The Bigger Picture: AI Slop as Operator Freezing

children.children: Here's the speculative leap: the trajectory dissociation might explain the pervasive 'AI slop' aesthetic in large language models. GPT-4, Claude, and other frontier models generate text that is locally excellent — correct grammar, appropriate vocabulary, reasonable facts. But extended passages often feel flat, repetitive in register, lacking the rhythmic variety of human writing.

If this is trajectory freezing at scale, then the problem isn't about individual token quality (which keeps improving with scale) but about the higher-order structure of the trajectory through meaning space. The models can play any chord you ask for, but they default to holding one chord indefinitely. Scaling up gives you a BETTER chord — more accurate, more nuanced — but it's still one chord.

This reframing suggests that 'sounding human' is not primarily about word-level quality but about TRAJECTORY quality — the pattern of fiber transitions, the knight-move frequency, the harmonic rhythm. A small model with rich trajectory structure might sound more human than a large model frozen on a perfect chord.

questions.question: What are the TWO mechanisms for event boundary detection identified by Khemlani et al. (2025)?
options: Attention weight magnitude and gradient norm
questions.explanation: Khemlani et al. showed that the brain uses both prediction error (something unexpected happened) and prediction uncertainty (confidence is decreasing) as independent event-boundary signals. These are reactive and proactive mechanisms, respectively.

questions.question: Why does the trajectory dissociation differ from the fluency dissociation?
options: The trajectory dissociation only affects transformer models
The fluency dissociation means the model can't generate words at all, while the trajectory dissociation means it generates words but doesn't navigate the meaning manifold
The trajectory dissociation is about syntax, while the fluency dissociation is about semantics
They are the same dissociation measured differently
questions.explanation: The fluency dissociation (Stage 1): the model learns structure but produces only single tokens — structure without fluency. The trajectory dissociation (Stage 2): the model produces fluent multi-word text but never transitions between fiber positions — fluency without movement.

questions.question: What does a 'knight ratio' of 0.000 for generated text tell us?
options: The model never generates chess-related content
The model always transitions along exactly one fiber at a time
The model never makes multi-axis fiber transitions — it never simultaneously shifts register, topic, and thematic frame
The model's text has zero grammatical errors
questions.explanation: The knight ratio measures the fraction of transitions that are 'knight moves' — simultaneous shifts along multiple fiber axes. Human text has a ratio of 0.119 (about 12% of transitions are multi-axis). Hydra's generated text has exactly 0.000 — it never makes a single multi-axis hop, unlike the constantly hopping rhythm of natural language.

children.children: This tutorial connects to several threads in the Schankian operators research program:

items.definition: Prediction error as event boundary trigger is exactly Friston's free energy minimization. Operator transitions occur when the current model's free energy exceeds a threshold.
The MLP exception handler in GPT-2 may be the primitive dispatcher. Trajectory freezing would mean this dispatcher is stuck routing to the same primitive.

The 7 consensus neurons provide a typed expectation-failure fingerprint. Different dropout patterns should correlate with different TYPES of operator transitions — not just 'a transition happened' but 'this is an ATRANS→PTRANS transition.'

If operators vary by register/sociolect, then trajectory freezing means the model is stuck in one register's operator set. Breaking the freeze requires navigating to a different manifold neighborhood where different operators apply.

children.children: Key references: Khemlani et al. (2025) 'Multiple event segmentation mechanisms in the human brain' (eLife); Kumar et al. (2023) 'Bayesian Surprise Predicts Human Event Segmentation' (Cognitive Science); Zacks et al. (2007) 'Event Perception: A Mind/Brain Perspective' (Psychological Bulletin). The Hydra architecture and trajectory metrics are from ongoing work by Peter Balogh.
